```
np_data = np.random.randn(5, 5, 5)
var = K.variable(np_data, dtype=np_data.dtype)
>>> np.array_equal(K.eval(var[:, 1, :]), np_data[:,1,:])
True
>>> np.array_equal(K.eval(var[:, -1, :]), np_data[:,-1,:])
True
>>> np.array_equal(K.eval(var[:, -2, :]), np_data[:,-2,:])
True
>>> np.array_equal(K.eval(var[:, -3, :]), np_data[:,-3,:])
True
>>> np.array_equal(K.eval(var[:, -4, :]), np_data[:,-4,:])
True
>>> np.array_equal(K.eval(var[:, -5, :]), np_data[:,-5,:])
True
```

We should probably implement some caching here.  There's only one place that ``self._keyword`` is called, but it gets called a LOT.  By writing the property this way, we'll end up re-compiling the regex on almost every single line of the config file being parsed.  Admittedly, I haven't done any profiling, so it's possible that this is just something that seems bad but wouldn't matter in practice.

With that being said, it's an easy enough fix.  Instead of

```
return re.compile(...)
```

We can just say

```
if not hasattr(self, '__keyword'):
    self.__keyword = re.compile(...)
return self.__keyword
```

That will ensure that the regex only gets compiled the first time.

['chunk']  should be a list of *some* of the public rooms (python dicts, not raw json).

From looking [at the spec](https://matrix.org/docs/spec/client_server/r0.2.0.html#get-matrix-client-r0-publicrooms), there is a whole load of information we're just ignoring which would be useful to almost any type of application, because then we don't have to send off a separate request to find out more information. *Ideally*, it would be nice to write a class that each room in the chunk can fill so developers get nice objects to work with. Something like ``PublicRoomListing``. I don't mind if you choose to just return the chunk however.

The other thing you need to be careful of is that it doesn't return all the rooms, you'd need to paginate to retrieve the lot so that's something we shouldn't be ignoring. Potentially a user could be testing this and getting confused as to why some of their rooms aren't coming up. Picking matrix.org as an example, it's *very* likely you're only going to get a tiny subset of the avaliable public rooms so I suggest you add a argument that allows people to paginate.



['chunk']  should be an object which contains information about a room.
Ideally the api module will return this as the raw object and the client module will return a Room object. The latter you've not implemented here so I wouldn't worry about that for now.

> Not true, since the value has a piece of randomness in it, also I don't see what is the value of a monotonic identifier, specially since we have the nonce.

It would only not me monothonic if it was ran more than once a second, which isn't expected (see that the random part is the last 3 digits, _random_ milliseconds, real seconds)). Nonce doesn't give time info, only order.

> What is the benefit?

The benefit is adding an information to the identifier (**when** the call started), without compromising the current behavior (random/non-collidable identifier).

If the old approach works as fine as this one, we can make the change for the benefits above, or if not, at least for consistency with webUI behavior, which will have time-based identifier generation.

In any case, this method is called only on missing identifier, and, in the practice, only webUI-generated (time-based) identifier will be used. In the future, with this idempotency approach to the other state-changing methods, we may even require client-provided `identifier`, which will get this auxiliary function removed.
